persons.arguments_against: BICKNELL is moderately common (10 officials, 2 bios), the bio carries only two initials H. J. with no birth year (age gate unchecked), there is no shared honour, and no edition co-occurrence bonus. A relative could in principle hold a magistracy. This fails because the single continuous stint reproduces the bio's dated career step for step, including a specific place and a timed role change. Bio 'Police magistrate, Kingston, Jamaica, Mar., 1860' matches the staff list's Police Magistrate, and specifically 'Police Magistrate, Kingston' in 1883. Bio 'R.M., St. Catherine, April, 1888' / 'resident magistrate ... April, 1888' matches the stint's transition from Police Magistrate to Resident Magistrate in 1889-90 — the right office change at the right time. Initials H. J. and colony Jamaica agree, and tenure (1877-90) sits inside the bio's span (magistrate from 1860, R.M. from 1888). A namesake would not replicate both the Kingston police magistracy and the 1888 shift to resident magistrate.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second H. J. Bicknell in the Jamaica magistracy, splitting the police-magistrate and resident-magistrate phases between two men.

notes: One continuous Jamaica judicial career matching the bio's specific posts (Police Magistrate at Kingston, then Resident Magistrate from 1888/89) and place; two-initial match. No honour or edition co-occurrence, but the place-specific position match plus the correctly-timed role change make it certain.
